body:
Your homeowner insurance renewal notice arrives 30–60 days before your policy expires. Most homeowners glance at the new premium, cringe slightly, and pay it. This is almost always a mistake.

Renewal time is your best opportunity to review your coverage, catch gaps, and potentially save significant money by shopping the market. Here's what to review.

## 1. Has Your Dwelling Coverage Kept Pace With Construction Costs?

Construction costs in the New York metro area have increased dramatically over the past several years. Your dwelling coverage (the amount needed to rebuild your home from scratch) should be updated annually.

If your policy was written 3–5 years ago and you haven't updated the dwelling limit, you may be significantly underinsured. Review your policy's replacement cost value and compare it to current construction cost estimates for your area.

## 2. Are Your Major Improvements Covered?

Did you renovate your kitchen, add a bathroom, finish the basement, or make other significant improvements since your last renewal? These improvements increase your home's replacement cost. If you didn't notify your insurer, these improvements may not be covered.

Contact your broker before renewal to ensure all improvements are reflected in your coverage.

## 3. Review Your Personal Property Coverage

Homeowner policies typically cover personal property at 50–70% of your dwelling limit. But this is a blanket coverage — it won't fully replace high-value items like jewelry, fine art, electronics, or musical instruments without a separate scheduled endorsement.

Review any high-value items you've acquired and consider adding scheduled personal property coverage.

## 4. Check Your Deductible

A higher deductible means lower premium. If you've built up savings since your policy was written, it may make sense to raise your deductible and pocket the premium savings.

However, be careful with hurricane or wind/hail deductibles, which are often separate and expressed as a percentage of dwelling coverage (e.g., 2% of $500,000 = $10,000 out-of-pocket).

## 5. Do You Need Flood Insurance?

Standard homeowner policies exclude flood damage. If you're in a FEMA flood zone — even a low-risk zone — flood insurance is worth considering. Flooding in Queens, Flushing, and other parts of NYC can happen even in non-designated flood zones.

Flood insurance is available through the National Flood Insurance Program (NFIP) or private carriers. Call us to discuss your risk level.

## 6. Shop the Market

Insurance loyalty is often penalized, not rewarded. Many carriers offer introductory discounts to new customers that erode over time. Shopping your renewal against other carriers is the most reliable way to ensure you're getting a competitive rate.

As an independent broker, Peerless Brokerage shops your homeowner coverage across 30+ carriers at every renewal — at no cost to you.

## Call Before Your Renewal Date

Contact us at (718) 799-0472 at least 30 days before your renewal date. We'll review your current policy, identify any coverage gaps, and compare quotes from multiple carriers. There's no cost and no obligation.
title: Home Insurance Renewal Checklist: What to Review Before Auto-Renewing
author: Peerless Brokerage Team
excerpt: Your home insurance renewal notice arrives and most people just pay it. That's a mistake. Here's what to review before renewing — and why switching carriers at renewal can save you hundreds.
